segments.text: Part 3A:Chapter 13 Introduction to Enzymes Dr. Shahir Rizk

Protein function
What do proteins do?
Carryout most physiological functions in the cell and the body
Main functions:
Structure, transport, signaling, immunity and catalysis of chemical reactions

Structural Proteins
support cellular structure/shape, provide mechanical support
Examples:
Keratin (hair & nails)
Actin & Myosin (muscles)
Tubulin: forms tubes known as microtubules (cell structure & division)

Transport proteins
Transport molecules to/from different parts of the cells/body
Function as ligand-binding proteins, binding is reversible
Examples:
Hemoglobin:
Transports oxygen/carbon
dioxide in blood
Transferrin:
Iron transport

Signaling
Sending and receiving biological signals from the environment
Triggering physiological responses
Example:
Hormones and receptors
Many hormones are proteins
All receptors are proteins
embedded in the membrane
Figure: Growth hormone (pink) binds to growth hormone receptor, which is a homodimer (green and blue) that spans the cell membrane (grey).

Immunity
Many proteins function to fight infection
Main example is antibodies (immunoglobulins)
Produced by immune cells (B-lymphocytes)
Bind to and neutralize foreign proteins (antigens)
Result is destruction of antigen by “macrophage”

Catalysis: Enzymes
Enzymes are proteins that catalyze a chemical reaction
Convert substrate(s) into product(s)
Enzymes are catalysts: (what is a catalyst?)
Accelerate the rate of a chemical reaction
Enzymes are not changed in the process
Reaction must be thermodynamically favored (spontaneous)

Thermodynamics and kinetics of chemical reactions
Take notes…

Virtually All Reactions in Cells Are Mediated by Enzymes
Enzymes catalyze thermodynamically favorable reactions, causing them to proceed at extraordinarily rapid rates
Enzymes provide cells with the ability to exert kinetic control over thermodynamic potentiality
Living systems use enzymes to accelerate and control the rates of vitally important biochemical reactions

Enzymes
How do enzymes accelerate the rate of a chemical reactions?
By lowering the activation energy (∆ G ‡ )
Enzymes DO NOT change the overall ∆G of a reaction

Virtually All Reactions in Cells Are Mediated by Enzymes
Figure 13.1 Reaction profile showing the large free energy of activation for glucose oxidation. Enzymes lower Δ G ‡ , thereby accelerating rate.

13.1 What Characteristic Features Define Enzymes?
Enzymes can accelerate reactions as much as 10 21 over uncatalyzed rates
Urease is a good example:
Catalyzed rate: 3x10 4 /sec
Uncatalyzed rate: 3x10 -10 /sec (~106 years)
Ratio (catalytic power) is 1x10 14
